coredata.dc:description: A rapid and accurate analysis of food quality and safety is essential for human safety and health. Optical sensors based on nanomaterials and nano-effects offer fast detection, simple operation, low cost, high specificity, and sensitivity, and therefore are widely used for food analysis. A variety of nanomaterials, including quantum dots, nano-porphyrin, nano-gold, nano-silver, upconversion nanoparticles, nanozymes, metal oxide nanomaterials and organic fluorescent molecules-based nanomaterials were successfully used as active components for optical sensors to detect harmful food contaminants as well as for quality and authenticity analyses. Most of these nano-sensors rely on either optical instruments or visual colorimetric detection. This paper reviews the detection mechanisms of nanomaterial-based optical sensors as well as the enhancement mechanisms of their specificity and sensitivity. Properties of various optically active nanomaterials, as well as detailed applications of these nano-sensors, are deliberated as well. Despite a variety of optical nanosensors reported in the literature for various applications, including rapid detection of food contaminants, quality and authenticity, their commercialization is still in its infancy stage.

head.ce:title: Nanomaterials as optical sensors for application in rapid detection of food contaminants, quality and authenticity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid and accurate analysis of food quality and safety is essential for human safety and health. Optical sensors based on nanomaterials and nano-effects offer fast detection, simple operation, low cost, high specificity, and sensitivity, and therefore are widely used for food analysis. A variety of nanomaterials, including quantum dots, nano-porphyrin, nano-gold, nano-silver, upconversion nanoparticles, nanozymes, metal oxide nanomaterials and organic fluorescent molecules-based nanomaterials were successfully used as active components for optical sensors to detect harmful food contaminants as well as for quality and authenticity analyses. Most of these nano-sensors rely on either optical instruments or visual colorimetric detection. This paper reviews the detection mechanisms of nanomaterial-based optical sensors as well as the enhancement mechanisms of their specificity and sensitivity. Properties of various optically active nanomaterials, as well as detailed applications of these nano-sensors, are deliberated as well. Despite a variety of optical nanosensors reported in the literature for various applications, including rapid detection of food contaminants, quality and authenticity, their commercialization is still in its infancy stage.

ce:section.ce:para: Judging by the popular expression - "hunger breeds discontentment", food is essential for our survival. Therefore, food safety is vital for public health and international trade. Issues related to food welfare can be divided into two categories: quality and safety. Food quality subjects can be further split into the intrinsic quality and external authenticity. The quality aspect includes nutrition value, functional characteristics, flavor, and taste, while authenticity implies the presence of illegal additives and contamination. Aspects related to food safety can also be divided into exogenous and endogenous ones. The exogenous food safety problem includes the presence of residues of pesticides, veterinary drugs, and heavy metals. Endogenous food safety issues include the presence of biological toxins and the harmful substances in products, which underwent thermal processing and fermentation. Therefore, food analysis focuses on the determination of food safety, quality, and authenticity [1–4].

Currently, food analysis is accomplished using gas chromatography (GC) [5] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6] (both alone and coupled with mass spectroscopy - GC-MS [7] and HPLC-MS [8], respectively) as well as near-infrared (NIR) [9], Raman [10], fluorescence [11] and ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) [12] spectroscopies. Chromatographic methods offer high separation efficiency and excellent detection performance, but they are costly, and suffer from time drift and inaccurate peak extraction problems. Thus, chromatographic methods are best suited for analyses performed at large companies and universities. Spectroscopic methods, like NIR, Raman, fluorescence and UV-vis spectra, are quick and straightforward to operate. They do not require sample destruction, but the peaks often overlap, causing poor detection specificity. Because of these limitations, chromatographic and spectroscopic methods are challenging to apply for rapid food safety analysis. Thus the research of rapid detection on the spot is crucial and necessary.

Biological methods such as immunoassay, enzyme inhibition, and bio-sensors are also widely used for rapid food analysis because they offer high sensitivity and strong specificity. However, these methods often output false-positive results due to the food matrix interference and thermal instability [13]. To solve these shortcomings, one can use nanomaterials, structures of which can be targeted to obtain specific functionality, such as surface effects, quantum sizes, quantum tunnels, and dielectric limits [14]. The usage of nanomaterials as active components for rapid sensors for food analysis offers fast detection, simple operation, low cost, high specificity, and sensitivity. Such optical sensors contain various quantum and carbon dots [15,16], nano-porphyrin [17], gold nanoparticles, silver nanoparticles[18], upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) [19], nanozymes [20], metal oxide nanomaterials [21] and organic fluorescent molecules-based nanomaterials [22]. The chromatographic methods have several problems such as time-consuming and requiring professional operation, and the spectroscopy methods have problems such as peak overlap and low accuracy. Compared to the these problems, the spectroscopy based on nanomaterials can meet the requirements of high accuracy and short detection time at the same time, so as to realize the requirements of simple, rapid and accurate detection in food safety. In addition, compared to biosensor, nanomaterials have higher thermal and chemical stability and can be applied to more complex and changeable food systems. This is great significance to the application in rapid detection of food contaminants, quality and authenticity, the stability and prosperity of the food market, and the health of the people.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials as active components for optical sensors

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are composed of semiconducting nanomaterials with particle sizes in the 1-10 nm range. Fluorescing semiconducting nanomaterials typically combine elements from the II − VI and III − V as well as IV − VI and V − VI groups [23]. QDs exhibit a strong electron confinement effect because their radii are below the bulk exciton Bohr radius. As a result, a ladder of discrete energy levels (and not energy bands) forms similar to the one in molecular systems [24,25]. Because of this, QDs demonstrate unique optical properties (Fig. 1 ). For example, QDs fluorescence emission spectra can be controlled (and even expanded to cover the entire visible light region) by changing their size and composition. QDs typically demonstrate wide excitation spectra often with significant Stokes shifts and narrow and symmetrical emission peaks. Last but not least, QDs demonstrate excellent stability upon exposure to light. QDs can be synthesized by a variety of methods, including hydrothermal, reflux condensation, organic solvent, and surface functionalization and modification techniques. And the hydrothermal method is frequently used in the field of food safety detection due to its simple method and good water solubility [26–29].

Carbon dots (CDs) belong to the QDs group and are typically synthesized from bio-materials. Therefore, CDs mostly contain C, H, O, and N. Thus, their raw materials are more abundant and more reliable than metallic QDs raw sources, have better security than metal QDs. CDs synthesized from graphene are called graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (Fig. 1). Researchers believe that the luminescence mechanism of CDs is related to the formation of carbon clusters [30], specific surface states [31], or based on dual photoluminescence (PL) emission [32]. CDs can also be doped by heterogeneous atoms to adjust their band gaps to increase the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and to improve or enhance CDs internal electronic structure [15,26–28,34,35].

Unique π-structure, ability to coordinate with various cations, outstanding electronic properties as well as photo- and electrochemical activities, molecular recognition ability, and biocompatibility, make porphyrin an ideal active material for biomimetic sensors [17]. Applications of porphyrin for such sensors were reported in the literature since the beginning of the year 2000 [36]. For example, chemical array sensors, constructed using a variety of metalloporphyrins using hydrophobic membranes as substrates, showed sensitive broad-spectrum identification capabilities [36]. In these sensors, metalloporphyrins reacted with the volatile organic compounds (VOCs), which caused the color change of metalloporphyrins (Fig. 1). The following compound identification was performed based on a fingerprint of these VOCs. Porphyrins are stabilized in their nano-states by surfactants, which also increases porphyrin solubility in food matrices for food rapid detection [16].

Gold (Au) and silver (Ag) nanoparticles (NPs), 1-100 nm in size, are the most widely used metallic NPs with unique optical properties due to the strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR) generated when these particles are in close proximity to each other. The electromagnetic radiation interaction with the AuNPs electrons located in the conduction band results in strong SPR because of the quantum size effects [37,38]. Because of SPR localization, AuNPs emission color can be changed by adjusting AuNPs morphology (shape and size) as well as distances between the NPs and the refractive index of the surrounding matrix (Fig. 1) [18,39]. Because of very high extinction coefficients (108-1010 L·mol−1 cm−1), AuNPs and AgNPs have a low limit of detection (LOD) according to Lambert-Beer law. AuNPs and AgNPs also have optical properties superior to other metal NPs, which is exceptionally beneficial to the visual detection of additives in trace amounts [40].

Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) which have unique upconversion luminescence (UCL) (an anti-stoke luminescence) are doped with rare earth elements, which consist of matrix material, rare earth activator and rare earth sensitizer [41,42]. When excited by long wavelength light, UCNPs have a short wavelength emission. The activator provides a luminescent center with abundant energy levels, such as Erbium (Er), Holmium (Ho) and Thulium (Tm). The sensitizer can absorbs energy and transfers energy to the activator. And Ytterbium (Yb) is usually used as sensitizer due to its special energy which can sense activator [43]. The UCL process is mainly the following three excitation modes (Fig. 2 ): 1) Excited state absorption (ESA) upconversion: The process is the same ion from the ground state to the excited state through continuous multipohoton absorption. 2) Energy transfer upconversion (ETU): When two excited state ions have similar energy, one can transfer energy to the other ion and return to a low energy state, and the other ion receives energy and transitions to a higher energy state. 3) Photon avalanche (PA): The process combined with ESA and ETU, and the energy transfer only occurs between the same kind of ions.

Moreover, some other nanomaterials are also used as sensors in food safety detection, such as nanozymes, metal oxide nanomaterials [21] and organic fluorescent molecules-based nanomaterials, which have their unique advantages. Nanozymes with catalytic ability can make the difference of spectrum and color after chemical reaction to amplify the detection signal by using fluorescence [44] or visual detection [45]. According to the catalytic mechanisms, the nanozymes can be mainly divided into oxidoreductases (e.g., oxidase, peroxidase, catalase, and superoxide dismutase) and hydrolases (e.g., nuclease and protease) [46]. Metal oxide nanomaterials not only have enzyme-like catalytic activity, but also are sensitive to surface chemistry with the increased percentage of surface metal, and surface peroxide species, thus they can used in visual detection. Therefore, metal oxide nanomaterials are usually used in the detection of special target detection by adsorbing or reacting with ligands [47]. Others, organic fluorescent molecules-based nanomaterials have the ability to combine spectral properties tunability and biocompatibility of small molecule organic fluorophores with brightness, chemical and colloidal stability of inorganic materials. [22] According to different targets, organic fluorescent molecular can be specially designed with highly selectivety for detecting targets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods and mechanisms of optical sensors

ce:section.ce:para:
Excellent luminescence properties of QDs make them an excellent choice for active materials for fluorescence-based sensors for the rapid detection of food ingredients. These sensors are typically based on fluorescence quenching occurring because of the nanomaterial reaction with the quencher. Fluorescence quenching modes are currently divided into static and dynamic ones. Static quenching occurs when a nonfluorescent ground-state complex forms as a result of QDs/quencher interaction. This complex returns to its ground state without photon emission upon light absorption. Characteristics of static quenching can be summarized as follows: 1) the fluorescence life of QDs does not change because of quenching agent presence; 2) the formation of the ground-state complex changes QDs absorption spectrum; 3) stability of the ground-state complex deteriorates at higher temperatures, which reduces the static quenching effect [48–50]. Dynamic quenching occurs when the excited state of QDs return to the ground one due to a quencher and a QDs collision and either energy or charge transfer between them [48]. Dynamic quenching mechanisms can also be based on Förster resonance energy and photoinduced electron transfers (FRET and PET, respectively), mechanisms of which are schematically shown in Fig. 3 [48]. FRET, first proposed in 1984, involves a donor and the acceptor dipole interactions [51]. FRET is effective to spectroscopically track the donor/acceptor interactions, which could be also used for sensing. According to the Föster theory of non-radiative energy transfer, FRET occurs when [52] 1) acceptor absorption and the donor emission spectra overlap; 2) dipoles of acceptor and donor are in specific orientations; 3) distance between the receptor and the donor is less than 10 nm. The rate of energy transfer between a donor and an acceptor (KD-A) separated by a distance r is expressed, as shown below [53]:

where I is the integral of a spectral overlap function, R0 is the Förster radium, B is a constant, and QD and τD are the donor fluorescence quantum yield and its exciton radiative lifetime, respectively, r is a distance (center-to-center) between donor and acceptor.

The photophysical mechanism of PET quenching of photoexcited QDs follows spatially-restricted close interactions between the electron donor-acceptor sites. Electron transfer of a photoexcited QDs typically participate in electron transfer via two general routes outlined in Fig. 3. Photoexcited QDs yield electron-hole pairs in the band gap. Under the presence of an electron acceptor possessing Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) energy level above the conduction band potential, conduction band electron transfer quenching to the acceptor units takes place. Similarly, under the presence of electron donors with Highest Occupied Molecular (HOMO) levels below the valence band potential, electrons transfer from the donor to the holes associated with the valence band [54]. IFE takes place when the quencher absorption spectra overlap with the excitation or emission spectrum of fluorescent materials. During IFE, quenching does not occur. Instead, an attenuation of the excitation beam or absorption of the emitted radiation by fluorescent materials or by the quencher in solution occurs [50], which leads to the reduced fluorescence. Thus, IFE is not classified as either static or dynamic quenching. Therefore, the absorption peaks of fluorescent materials would not change because no new complexes form [48]. In additional, some complexes and proteins in food and biological have fluorescence with short fluorescence lifetime, which interferes with fluorescence detection and reduces sensitivity. Nanomaterials with long fluorescence lifetime combined with time-resolved fluorescence technology can minimize the interference of instantaneous fluorescence, which can be used in the rapid detection of these foods with fluorescence interference [55].

The unique properties of AuNPs and AgNPs strongly rely on their size, shape, and aggregation degree (Fig. 1). All these morphological parameters can be easily tuned, which, in turn, will change their UV-vis absorption spectra, which is very beneficial for rapid food analysis using a combination of optical sensors containing AuNPs and AgNPs as active materials and UV-vis spectrophotometry [40]. This visual colorimetric method will replace the subjective analysis of a human eye by an impartial photoelectric qualitative or quantitative detection of food ingredients. According to the Lambert-Beer law, the electromagnetic wavelength range for these types of measurements is very wide: 190-760 nm.

Both AuNPs and AgNPs demonstrate surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) effect when reaching a specific size and proximity. SERS can be measured using a typical Raman spectrometer. Principals of Raman spectroscopy are based on the molecules’ ability to scatter electromagnetic radiation in the 40-4000 cm-1 range (Fig. 4 ). Typically, during Raman analysis, the samples are irradiated by monochromatic light, inelastic scattering of which provides information correlated to the vibrational modes of the chemical species that constitute the sample, resulting in a very selective technique [56]. SERS gained popularity during the last decade. It relies on analyte adsorption on the roughened surfaces of Ag and/or Au, which amplify Raman scattering of the analyte (by 3-6 orders of magnitude). Thus, even very small amounts of chemicals can be detected by SERS [57].

Compared with fluorescence, the energy of chemiluminescence is provided by the process of chemical reactions (Fig. 5 ). Chemiluminescent can be divided into direct luminescence and indirect luminescence. Different from direct luminescence, indirect luminescence has energy transfer process. According to the difference way of energy source, chemiluminscent can be divided into three types: Ordinary chemiluminescence, Biochemiluminescence (BCL) and Electrochemiluminescence (ECL). In general, chemiluminescence is based on organic structure, such as lumino, lucigenin, lophine, and oxalate peroxide esters, which can generate a product in the excited state through multiple oxidation reactions, while returning to the ground state emits the light in the shorter wavelength [58,59]. Some nanomaterials (AuNPs [60], AgNPs [61], nanosheet [62], MOFs [63]) with enzyme-like catalytic activity, is often used as a catalyst for chemiluminescence reactions to increase the detection signal and reduce the detection limit (Fig. 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in rapid detection of food contaminants, quality and authenticity

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are used to prevent or control plant diseases and damages caused by pests, including weeds. Pesticides, currently used in large-scale agriculture and home gardens, can be both natural and synthetic chemicals. Over the years, the usage of pesticides by farmers and gardeners became increasingly extensive, which forced the governments worldwide to introduce the upper limits of pesticide residues for agricultural products [64]. Pesticides remaining in food are typically ingested during consumption through digestive and/or respiratory systems as well as skin. Analysis of the remaining pesticide in agricultural products is one way to address these contamination issues. To address this, rapid and accurate detection methods of food ingredients in small quantities are needed. This will help not only governmental regulators but also consumers to be aware of food safety. Recently developed optical sensors based on nanomaterials are well suited for these purposes; they are also more convenient and even more accurate than traditional bulky methods (such as HPLC, GC-MS, etc.) yet can achieve quick and specific in-field analysis of harmful chemicals and their levels [10,18,65].

Among such optical methods, fluorescence quenching detection is the most common and simple. Pesticides’ direct interaction with nanomaterials (such as QDs) results in fluorescence quenching, the degree of which can be then converted to the pesticide content. Ng et al. [66] fabricated a fluorescent sensor based on CDs to detect paraoxon-ethyl and achieved a LOD of 0.22 ± 0.02 μmol·L−1. Another fluorescent sensor, based on N-doped CDs and their IFE, was used to detect methyl parathion at LOD of 1.87 ppb [67]. When used by themselves, QDs alone can also be used for pesticide detection, but they often demonstrate poor anti-interference ability. Therefore, QDs are often combined with other nanomaterials to be then used for fluorescent sensing. These composite materials include QDs-QDs [65], QDs-AgNPs [68], etc. Different quenching degrees of these composite QDs made it possible to detect multicomponent pesticides with high sensitivity and selectivity [65]. One such example is the detection of glyphosate by AgNPs/QDs composites [68].

Detection methods based on ratiometric fluorescence are more sensitive than those based on fluorescence quenching. Li et al. [69] demonstrated a ratiometric fluorescence response of CDs/Cu-based sensors triggered by Cu2+ presence. The resulting dual emission was used to detect inert thiophanate-methyl (TM). TM recognition by CDs/Cu was based on π−π stacking chelation of Cu2+, which induced synchronous fluorescent quenching of the dual emitting CDs/Cu. The mechanism of this process was based on transfers 1) of the intramolecular protons from the excited state and 2) of the ligand-to-metal charge [69]. QDs can also be quenched by nanoporphyrins [18]. Sensors containing QDs-nanoporphyrins as active materials recover after organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) addition because of the strong confinement between OPPs and nanoporphyrin. Therefore, nanoporphyrins are often added to the active sensing materials to improve their specificity. For example, FRET between QDs and 5, 10, 15, 20- Tetra (4-pyridyl) porphyrin (TPyP) resulted in QDs fluorescence quenching [18,70]. However, FRET was stopped when OPPs were added because of their interaction with TPyP. The fluorescence intensity of TPyP decreased as OPP concentration in the system increased [70]. This mechanism was taken advantage of during the implementation of a paper-based sensor containing composite QDs: their interaction with TPyP amplified the signal and produced different color responses. This approach used partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLSDA) and was very successful during fingerprint analysis in the complex matrix [18]. In addition, UCNPs are often used as ratiometric fluorescence materials due to their unique upconversion luminescence properties. Saleh et al. fabricated a NaYF4: Yb, Er with a red and green emission (659 and 545 nm) after emitted under 980 nm, when metribuzin exists, the emission in 659 nm will be quenched due to an IFE.[71] Zeng et al. fabricated a NaYF4：Yb, Tm have a dual emission (803 nm and 475 nm). The emission in 475 nm quenched due to LRET effect between UCNPs and the pesticide, when OPPs exists, the blue emission (475 nm) will recovered. Thus, it can be used for the detection of OPPs.

For improved specificity and sensitivity, active nanomaterials in sensors are often combined with enzymes, which can catalyze certain reactions, providing enhanced selectivity of the biosensors to particular pesticides. One drawback is demanding enzyme storage conditions. Usage of a composite material containing enzymes and NPs was already demonstrated for pesticide detection, especially organophosphorus-based ones. The most commonly used enzyme for these applications is acetylcholinesterase (AChE) found in the nervous system [72]. OPPs could effectively inhibit the production of H2O2 and thiocholine by destroying the AchE activity. Then, the fluorescence of GQDs (typically quenched by H2O2 [73]) and of CDs (typically quenched by Fe3+ formed by oxidation of Fe2+ by H2O2 [74]) will hold turn on in the presence of OPPs. Similarly, without thiocholine, the fluorescence of CDs (typically quenched by 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoic acid formed due to the decomposition of 5,5-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) by thiocholine [75]) is also suppressed, so it can be used for the detection of enzyme inhibitor, like OPPs and carbamates pesticide.

The best specificity of rapid pesticide detection was reported for a sensor based on a combination of NPs, aptamer, and molecular imprinting polymer (MIP). Specific reaction, similar to the one between an antigen and antibody, between tebuconazole (TEB) and a monoclonal antibody (mAb) under the presence of hapten (in which the p-chloro substituent of the TEB molecule was replaced with a long-chain carboxylic acid) was monitored by ELISA [76]. The mAb showed high sensitivity and specificity against TEB with low cross-reactivity (<0.01 %) under the presence of several analogs of triazole fungicides. Thus, mAb was incorporated into fluorescence immunochromatographic test strip assay, also containing QDs beads for rapid and accurate detection of TEB in agricultural products [76]. In additional, MIP-modified CDs were also used as fluorophores in the optical sensors for thiabendazole (TBZ) determination. MIP-functionalization of CDs was performed by first fabricating a silica sell around CDs by applying tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) using a reverse microemulsion technique. MIPs were then obtained using TBZ as a template as well as TEOS and 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane as a crosslinker (between MIP and CDs) and a functional monomer, respectively [77]. Furthermore, consider with selectivity and sensitivity, MIT and ratiometric fluorescence are usually applied simultaneously. Chen et al used MIPs based turn on ratiometric fluorescence for the detection of 2,4-D by using QDs [78]. The probe which may cause the different of color can be used as a colorimetric probe to visual detected pesticides. Wu et al. developed a quantitative method for the visual detection of benzothiostrobin in strawberry by using the conjugates of CdSe/ZnS QDs and anti-benzothiostrobin antibody based fluorescence lateral flow test strip [79]. The results were visualized with the unaided eye with using ultraviolet protective goggles under UV lamp with a detection limit of 25 μg/L.

Antibiotics are present in a variety of veterinary drugs. Almost half of the antibiotics consumed worldwide were used in husbandry [80]. These antibiotics mainly include tetracyclines (TCs), chloramphenicol (CAP), and aminoglycosides (AAs). Consuming meat products containing these and other antibiotics might lead to the intestinal bacterial disbalance, allergic reactions, and bacterial resistance.

Although nanomaterials are currently not used for antibiotic detection as widely as for pesticide analysis, research on their applications to antibiotics is rapidly developing. One of the simplest and most popular methods is again based on fluorescence quenching. Wang et al. [81,82] fabricated a fluorescent sensor using MoS2 NPs to detect TCs. Mechanism of the fluorescence quenching under the TC presence was because of a combination of the inner filter effect and the electron transfer. A similar TC sensor containing GQDs and Eu3+ was fabricated, taking advantage of the strong interactions between Eu3+ and TCs. Upon excitation at 360 nm and without TC presence, only a strong emission of GQDs was observed for this composite system, while the emission of Eu3+ was very weak. However, under the TC presence, GQDs fluorescence was quenched, while the fluorescence of Eu3+ was enhanced [82].

AuNPs and AgNPs were also successfully used as active materials for sensors to detect TCs. These sensors were based on SPR [83] and SERS [84]. Lee et al. developed a new assay for detection of trace amounts of TC, which involved a DNA aptamer and an antibody pair. The antibody probe was covalently bonded to AuNPs and integrated into a surface sandwich assay combined with an SPR Au chip modified by the TC-specific aptamer. Excellent selectivity was achieved relative to other TC derivatives [83]. Juan et al. used the SERS of AgNPs to detect oxytetracycline (OTC) [84]. Even at ppb-contents of the antibiotic, the corresponding frequencies were clearly observed, and the compound could be distinctly differentiated from the honey matrix. OTC adsorption on AgNPs occurred through the oxygen atoms. Such excellent sensor performance eliminated the need for an extraction and sample manipulation procedures to extract it from the food samples for further detection. To verify that the SERS signal indeed belonged to OTC, density functional theory was applied to simulate the OTC spectrum at these conditions. Both spectra, simulated and experimental ones, showed excellent agreement [84].

Usage of sensor arrays can provide even more detailed data on the antibiotics present in food. For example, a dual-channel fluorescence sensor array based on CDs was capable of detecting TCs taking advantages of their distinct fluorescence patterns accurately. The quantitative detection was performed by monitoring the CDs fluorescence intensity change. By enhancing the sensors using chemometrics including machine learning techniques, linear discriminant analysis and support vector machine, and four different TC types were distinguished at levels as low as 1.0 μmol L−1 [85].

Analyses of CAP and AAs using sensors containing NPs as active materials were also reported in the literature. Colorimetric aptasensors containing AuNPs were used to detect CAP [86–88]. In one case, an intact aptamer, as a part of a terminal-lock, acted as a molecular recognition element. The same sensor also contained a short oligonucleotide, chosen to complement the aptamer terminal fragments and which served as a locker probe [86]. By forming an aptamer/target complex, the LP abandons the ATL and adsorbs on the AuNPs, stabilizing them against salt-induced aggregations [86]. Taghdisi et al. [87] also developed a colorimetric sandwich aptasensor, which forms when no chloramphenicol is present and is revealed by the bright red color. In the presence of a target, functionalized AuNPs could not bind to 96-well plates, resulting in a faint red color. Similarly, Taghdisi et al. designed a colorimetric aptasensors using AuNPs suspended in water and double-stranded DNA (dsDNA). Without streptomycin, aptamer/FAM-labeled complementary strand dsDNA was stabilized, but AuNPs were aggregated, which resulted in a color change (from red to blue) and strong fluorescence signal. Under the streptomycin presence, the aptamer bonded to its target and FAM-labeled complementary strand adsorbed on AuNPs, which separated aggregated AuNPs. The color of the resulting AuNPs suspension became wine-red color. At the same time, the fluorescence of the FAM-labeled strand was quenched by the AuNPs [88]. Cao et al. constructed a novel homogeneous aptamer with an “off-on” signal using fluorescence assay for CAP detection in food. The mechanism of this sensor was based on FRET. The vesicle nanotracer composite probe, prepared using labeled single-stranded DNA binding protein (SSB) on liposome-CdSe/ZnS quantum dot (SSB/L-QDs) complexes, was fabricated using aptamer labeled nano-gold (Au-Apt) and SSB/LQDs. The resulting system used a specific affinity of SSB towards the aptamer. Upon CAP addition to the system, Au-Apt aptamer preferentially bonded with CAP and detached from the composite probe. This process corresponds to the “off” signal of the SSB/L-QDs tracer [89]. Another example of a sensor array is an AA-sensing platform based on AuNPs. The presence of AAs triggers the DNA detachment from AuNPs forcing salt-induced AuNPs aggregations of various degrees. This technique was used to analyze with 100% accuracy five different AAs using pattern recognition techniques, the Fisher linear discriminant, and hierarchical cluster analyses (FLD and HCA, respectively [90]). However, this method demonstrated poor specificity, which could be improved by combining QDs with MIPs.

Thus, Liang et al. [91] described the fabrication of a fluorescent aptamer functionalized with MIP to be used for selective kanamycin detection. The role of MIP was to recognize the aptamer and to imprint cavities for further fluorescent sensing of kanamycin. An environmentally friendly, efficient “thiol-one” click-reaction, efficient even when conducted under the mild conditions, was utilized to incorporate the aptamer into the MIP matrix.

The aptasensors for analysis of multiple antibiotics at the same time were also reported [92–94]. One example is a novel colorimetric aptasensor based on an ss-DNA fragment used to coordinate AuNPs, which also prevented their aggregation. This multifunctional aptamer was adsorbed on the AuNPs and bonded with antibiotics acting as a molecular switch. When one antibiotic was added, a certain aptamer fragment bonded with it, which caused its dissociation. Thus, a color change associated with aggregation and dispersion of AuNPs can be used as a signal for the detection of antibiotics [93]. Song et al. assembled a multicolor QDs-based immunofluorescence assay into an array. They achieved simultaneous, sensitive, and visual detection of TC, penicillin G (PC-G), and streptomycin (SM) in milk. Antibodies for these antibiotics were attached to separate QDs groups, each of which possessed emissions with different wavelengths. The resulting QDs-Ab detection probes were then used in a competitive fluorescent immunoassay performed in antigen-coated microtiter plate wells [94].

MIPs were also reported in combination with nanomaterials the rapid detection of antibiotics. Sadeghi et al. prepared Mn-doped ZnS QDs functionalized with the florfenicol MIP. This composite material was fabricated using a sol-gel surface imprinting approach using 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane and tetraethoxysilane as a functional monomer and as the crosslinker, respectively. The resulting active Mn:ZnS QDs@MIP material was incorporated into an optical sensor for florfenicol detection. By comparing the performance of this active material with an Mn:ZnS QDs covered with non-imprinted material without florfenicol (FF) presence, it was deduced that fluorescence of the Mn:ZnS QDs@MIP increased as FF concentration increased. Fluorescence of Mn:ZnS QDs@MIP intensified under the presence of the target molecule and showed a linear correlation with its concentration in the 30-700 μmol L−1 range. The detection limit for this sensor with regards to florfenicol was 24 μmol L−1. To demonstrate practical applications of this sensor, it was successfully used for FF analysis in meat [95]. Razmjou et al. prepared a ratiometric fluorescent sensor containing CDs (with different emission wavelengths and acting as dual fluorophores) and a mesoporous MIP (acting as a receptor). This B/YCDs@mMIP probe was to detect penicillin-G (PNG) in milk. PNG presence activated the yellow emission of CDs, which was consequently neutralized by the analyte. At the same time, the blue emission of the other CDs group remained almost constant. Thus, the overall color change of the probe switched from yellow to blue. The sensor demonstrates a linear response to 1-32 nmol·L−1 of PNG with 0.34 nmol·L−1 detection limit and excellent recognition specificity over corresponding molecular analogs [96].

The presence of heavy metals in the environment has serious consequences causing health problems due to their accumulation and non-biodegradability. This issue resonates with numerous research groups seeking solutions to simple and sensitive heavy metal analysis methods. The examples below list some, but obviously not all, of the problems associated with elevated heavy metal contents to emphasize the seriousness of the heavy metal presence in the environment.

One of the most toxic and hazardous heavy metals is mercury (Hg) [97]. Its presence in the bloodstream causes motion disorders and coronary heart problems. Another very toxic element is copper (Cu), high physiological contents of which burden liver and other organ functions causing metabolic disorders, liver cirrhosis, etc. [98]. Other metals are also toxic for physiological processes. Thus, the excessive Ag+ intake causes argyria as well as nervous, renal, immune, and cardiovascular system damage [99]. Excessive amounts of free Fe3+ can result in the presence of hydroxyl radicals, which are damaging to cell membranes and DNA. Iron cations can also chelate with various proteins inhibiting their functions [100]. Because of the serious issues associated with these heavy metal cations, several methods of Hg2+ [101–103], Cu2+ [104,105], Ag+ [99,106] and Fe3+ [117,118] detection were developed and reported in the literature. Among them, fluorescence quenching is the mainly method for detecting heavy metal ions. The S-V equation based on the fulorescence quenching between nanomaterials and heavy metal ions is the basis of quantitative detection. For the more sensitive detection, ratiometric fluorescence methods are used for detection heavy metal ions. What’s more, the colorimetric visualization based on the fluorescence quenching and ratiometric fluorescence methods can make a faster detection.

Heavy metal ions are reactive enough to bind with pure and functionalized QDs directly and quench their fluorescence. The quenching degree can thus be used to measure their concentrations. Hg2+ reacts with AA-enhanced CDs [109], N-doped CDs [110], N-doped GQDs [101], core-shell CDs [111], etc. Fu et al. [109] and Chen et al. [110] prepared AA-enhanced and N-doped CDs quenchable by Hg2+, respectively. Abdolmohammad-Zadeh et al. prepared an N-doped GQDs, chemiluminescence (CL) emission of which could be induced by the presence of strongly oxidizing K3Fe(CN)6. They studied the CL emission mechanism of the resulting material to understand how Hg2+ affects CL of g-CNQDs-K3Fe(CN)6 system. Using this information, a simple and sensitive sensor was fabricated for Hg2+ detection in water [101]. Core-shell CDs also strongly interact with Hg2+ in aqueous medium forming aggregates. Carboxyl surface groups of CDs bind Hg2+ facilitating electron transfer from CDs to Hg2+ [111].

Ratiometric fluorescence methods are typically more sensitive than methods based on fluorescence quenching. Thus, Zhao et al. developed a nanohybrid dual-emission system containing CDs to detect Hg2+. The resulting sensor emitted bands at 470 and 678 nm when excited only once. Fluorescence of 678 nm was quenched under the Hg2+ presence, only slightly altering the intensity of the 470 nm band [102]. Sensor arrays for heavy metal detection were also developed to take advantage of the synergy between different materials. For instance, Xu et al. synthesized CDs soluble (CDs-A) and insoluble (CDs-B) in ethanol. Fluorescence of both CDs-A and CDs-B was quenched by ClO− and Hg2+ presence. However, CDs-A were more sensitive towards ClO−, while CDs-B towards Hg2+.

AuNPs and AgNPs were also reported as parts of the active materials in sensors used for Hg2+ detection. Wang et al. developed a SERS substrate based on AgNPs by immobilizing T-component oligonucleotide probes labeled. The resulting sensor responded to thymine/Hg2+ bond formation changing the previous flexible single strand of oligonucleotide probe into a rigid one with a double chain [112].

Wang et al. reported sensors for Cu2+ detection [105]. First, they synthesized fluorescing CDs using hydrothermal carbonization of natural kelp juice. The resulting CDs, functionalized using polyethyleneimine (PEI), were then conjugated with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC). The resulting CDs-FITC composites were then used as active sensing materials for optical analysis pH and Cu2+. Detection, based on visual and radiometric FL, of pH in glass bottles and yogurt, respectively, as well as for latent fingerprint and leaf imaging detection of Cu2+. The same probe was also used dual-signal FL sensing of Cu2+ in yogurt and human serums. Fu et al. developed a sample-in-answer-out paper-based assay for Ag+ detection [99] using in situ Ag+-triggered autocatalysis as a signal amplifier. The read-out of this sensor did not require any instruments. The analytical device was a 3D origami paper-based one combined with a nanopore membrane (NM). The NM pores were partially blocked by the analytes, which prolonged the flow time of a colored reagent. Zhu et al. developed a fluorescent technique to analyze Fe3+ ethylenediaminetetraacetate. The sensors contained photoluminescent CDs and F− ions to mask Fe3+: Fe3+ ethylenediaminetetraacetate selectively quenched the CDs PL when F- was present in the system. The presence of free Fe3+ and Fe2+ or other carboxylic acids did not affect CDs emission. The sensing mechanism relied on ligand-tailored electron transfer from CDs to Fe3+ [107]. Lee et al. synthesized very stable fluorescent N-doped CDs (FNCQDs) (using Phyllanthus acidus (P. acidus) and ammonia) with high QY [108]. They used them as active material to fabricate probes for Fe3+ detection. The resulting sensor response linearly correlated with the Fe3+ content. Thus, these FNCQDs were indeed useful for label-free accurate and selective Fe3+ detection in the 2-25 μmol L−1 range with 0.9 μmol L−1 detection limit. Thus, water-soluble N-doped CDs with reliable and stable fluorescence are indeed promising for the diverse sensing applications, including but not limited to label-free Fe3+ detection, fluorescent ink, cellular imaging, and so on.

The aptasensors for the simultaneous detection of more than one heavy metal were also developed [103,104,106,113]. Typically, when single QDs responds to the presence of a variety of diverse heavy metal cations, poor selectivity is observed. However, under the presence of a masking agent, the simultaneous detection of several heavy metal cations can be accomplished. For example, Wu et al. designed water-soluble CdTe QDs quenchable by Ag+, Hg2+ or Cu2+ ions and functionalized their surface with sodium diethyldithiocarbamate (DDTC), NH4OH and 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol masking agents to neutralize Ag+/Cu2+, Ag+/Hg2+ and Hg2+/Cu2+, respectively, to selectively detect Ag+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ [106].

Except for single quantum dots, sensor array is more convenience for the simultaneous detection. Fan et al. developed sensor arrays using four groups of Mn-doped ZnS QDs, separately functionalized with N-Acetylcysteine, citric acid, mercaptopropionic acid, and triammonium-N-dithiocarboxyiminodiacetate. The functionalized QDs quenched Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, and Cd2+ at a different degree, which allowed the sensor array to exhibit a unique pattern of fluorescence variations at 30 pg/ml, easily discriminated by principal component analysis (PCA) [103]. Hormozi-Nezhad et al. developed a sensor array based on anti-aggregation and competitive interaction of citrate-capped AuNPs and Hg2+, Ag+, Fe3+, and Pb2+ with cysteine, melamine, and arginine [106]. In addition to the detection with instrument, colorimetric is faster and more convenient. Chitosan capped AuNPs were fabricated for the detection of Hg2+, the LOD is 0.1 ppb [114]. Compared to inorganic mercury, organic mercury is more toxic. Chen et al. fabricted a diethyldithiocarbamate capped AuNPs for the detection of mercury, and by virtue of the masking effect of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, the organic mercury can be detected.

Biotoxins are produced by physiological processes of bacteria, viruses, fungi, rickettsia, protozoa, and of some plants and can cause illness or death of humans and animals if consumed [115]. Biotoxin effects can be minor (e. g., as from Ergot alkaloids), acute (e. g., as from Shiga toxin) or lethal (e. g., as from Botox) [116]. Biotoxin contamination can be hazardous for living organisms, including humans and large animals, and can affect the economy worldwide. Thus, Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), together with other developed countries, established strict limits on biotoxin contents in food.

Patulin toxin, produced by molds in fruits, can cause gastrointestinal disorders as well as the edema and bleeding of the organs in animals. Khataee et al. used AgNPs/Zn-based metal-organic frameworks (MOF) nanocomposite (AgNPs@ZnMOF) possessing excellent peroxidase-like activity as an MIP support [117]. The resulting sensing platform was then applied for patulin detection. Electron capturing features of patulin reduced the catalytic activity of AgNPs@ZnMOF. This property was used for an assay design for patulin detection. This assay relied on a specific identifying feature of MIP, peroxidase-like activity of AgNPs@ZnMOF, and overall sensitivity of this fluorescence probe [117]. Mn-doped ZnS QDs modified (using surface molecular imprinting sol-gel process) by 6-hydroxynicotinic acid (6-HNA) were also tested with regards to the selective phosphorescent determination of patulin. The resulting system demonstrated a higher selectivity, adsorption capacity, and mass transfer rate than a similar system without non-imprinted polymers [118].

Zearalenone (ZEN) is an estrogenic mycotoxin commonly found in cereals and is more toxic than patulin. A colorimetric aptasensor based on the nontarget-induced walker was used to analyze the presence of ZEN. The active components of these sensors were AuNPs acting as catalytic exonuclease III (Exo III) and 4-nitrophenol used as colorimetric agents [119]. Similarly, Wang et al. developed a fluoroimmuno-assay containing magnetic nanobeads (MNBs). The working mechanism of the resulting sensor was recognition of protein G binder: MNBs functionalized with G attached to the crystallizable region of immunoglobulin G (IgG) at a particular orientation. At the same time, the antibody binding sites were not damaged during this chemical coupling [120]. CDs encapsulated into MIP with quenchable fluorescence were also used as active materials to determine ZEN cereal [121]. AuNPs combined with UCNPs were also used as for the detection of ZEN. [122]

Aflatoxin (AF) is another very toxic substance present in grain-, peanut-, corn- and rice-containing foods. It was successfully detected by a sensor containing ZnS QDs encapsulated in MIP. The resulting MIP-QDs composite demonstrated high affinity and selectivity in relationship to AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, and AFG2 AFs [123]. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) was successfully monitored using a sensitive immunoassay containing CdTe/CdS/ZnS QDs [124] as well as an enzyme-free biosensor containing a catalytic DNA circuit [125]. Huang et al. used AuNPs in the presence of chitosan to obtain peroxidase-like properties according to the H2O2/3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine tests. This colorimetric assay used immunoreactions on the enzyme, which mimicked Au/chitosan composite membrane containing AuNPs with different emissions, which served as an output signal. The selectivity of this sensor was assessed using Staphylococcus aureus under the presence of Escherichia coli and Bacillus subtilis. 1.0 × 10−12 g·mL-1 of staphylococcal enterotoxin B was detected by a color changed observed by a naked eye [126].

Food cultivated in different ecological environments has different compositions, which, in turn, define their prices. For example, authentic traditional Chinese teas [127–129] and cereal vinegars [90], as well as certain wines [130], might require quality control to know in which exactly region they were produced (Fig. 6 and Table 2 ). Therefore, the development of fast and accurate quality control methods for products is essential for consumers and the global market in general.

Tea, the most popular and desired drink in the world, produced in a different location using different techniques, and might have different taste and efficacy as well as price. Because of its complex composition and presence of various chemicals (for example, polyphenol, caffeine, and theanine), QDs can be easily quenched by teas. As a result, there are numerous methods for tea analysis based on fluorescence quenching. A CDs-containing fluorescent probe was combined with PCA for the analysis of Dianhong black tea grade [127]. Similarly, Fu et al. [128] synthesized a novel fluorescent probe, containing CdTe QDs and operating based on the fluorescence “turn-off” model, and used it to differentiate 29 of green teas. PLSDA was used to discriminate against the teas with high sensitivity and specificity with a 100% recognition rate [128]. Single-compound QDs typically showed good quality control results during the test containing several kinds of teas. However, at the variety of tested tea increased, it more and more difficult for these sensors to distinguish the tea varieties. To solve these problems, Fu et al. developed double-compound QDs and used them to recognize 53 of authentic green teas using sensors based on the fluorescent “turn-off” model. Using PLSDA, the teas were recognized with high sensitivity, specificity, and a 100 % recognition rate for training and prediction sets [129]. A chemical sensor array based on nanozymes (Cu-ATP, Cu-ADP, Cu-AMP) was developed to discriminate between different teas. Chang et al. [131] successfully discriminated 12 samples with 100% accuracy through the phenomenon which different kinds of tea samples appeared in different areas in the canonical score plot with different response patterns.

Another successful sensing approach was based on the quenching/dequenching of emissions by the sample components [130]. The preparation procedure involved mixing the sample with fluorophores in 96-well plates and recording emissions by digital photography. After RGB splitting, the image data was normalized by PCA. The discrimination quality judged by the number of separate groups of points in the score plots was significantly improved when the image contrasts were processed by the software. To reinforce interactions with a wide range of markers, six very different fluorophores were used. Ru(bpy)3 2+ fluorophore could alone distinguish 16 different types of whiskey. The quality of RGB-based analysis was equal to or better than sensor performance based on spectrofluorimetry in the 400-700 nm range.

A sensor array based on pH-sensitive CdTe QDs coupled with chemometric method was capable of identifying 32 different Chinese cereal vinegars; the detection was quick yet accurate and sensitive. The mechanism of this sensor array depended on the presence of specific organic acids and melanoidins in these vinegars. Organic acids quenched the QDs fluorescence by enhancing their electron and resonance energy transfer. The melanoidin fluorescence is closely related to the brewing process and aging of these cereal vinegars. The vinegars were identified with 100% accuracy, even at 1000 time dilution using PLSDA. The array used only one QDs type was used instead of various organic dyes. This array probe was also successfully applied for other organic acid-rich products [90].

Authenticity confirmation of various food products includes adulteration discrimination, marker, and non-targeted detection (Fig. 6 and Table 2). Adulteration is likely to be illegally added to foods to either decrease prices, to make the product more available and/or efficient, or to obtain higher profits, such as in the case of malachite green [132] and melamine [133,134]. Malachite green addition gives a fresher and greener look to produce. To satisfy consumer demands to know whether the organic dye is present, a CDs-based sensor was developed for the malachite green detection [135]. Milk processing companies producing powdered infant formulas typically determine the protein level by analyzing total N content. The addition of N-rich melamine to milk increases its overall N content, which falsely enhancing its measured protein content. Consumption of melamine with food might cause kidney failure, stone formation and even death. Therefore, research and development dedicated to melamine determinations became very popular [133,134]. Functionalized AuNPs were successfully used for melamine; functionalization was necessary because melamine presence destabilized the AuNPs suspension leading to NP aggregation. One example includes the formation of an electrostatic layer containing citrate anions on AuNPs to stabilize their dispersion [135]. The citrate-capped AuNPs were modified with Triton X-100 to further stabilize the AuNPs suspension against high ionic strength and broad pH range conditions. Gorthi et al. used AgNPs to analyze melamine in milk by observing the color change during AgNPs bio-synthesis caused by the melamine presence [128]. The mechanism of the color change was based on melamine hydrogen-bonding with AA, which interfered with AgNPs formation [133,136].

Specific markers, such as functional or flavor components, are often used to confirm food authenticity. For example, the ferulic acid content is 95% of the total phenolic acid content in virgin argan oil; thus, it can be used as an adulteration marker to evaluate oil authenticity, for instance, using sensors based on AuNPs [137]. Nucleotide fragments are also effective markers. For example, a 27-nucleotide AluI fragment of swine cytochrome b gene was used to functionalize AuNPs to authenticate pork adulteration in meatballs. The sensor containing this active material was capable of detecting 1% pork in raw and cooked meatballs containing beef as well [138].

Authenticity markers are sometimes added to foods illegally to obtain false-positive results, which are all unpredictable and unable to perform targeted detection. Thus, non-target detection, which based on the detailed information of the detected object and classification algorithm, and can be used for the accurate identification of the detected object with unknown foreign components added, will has a greater prospect. Adulterated vegetable cooking oils were successfully discovered using a rapid optical sensor based on fluorescence quenching of CdSe/ZnS QDs without any sample preparation. The sensor detected the presence of ≥0.4% of refined oil in soybean oil within 2 min [139]. In another study, to process the sensing data effectively, a "turn-off" fluorescent probe containing QDs was combined with one-class partial least squares (OCPLS) calculation to screen the presence of extraneous additive in orange juice [140] and honey [141]. OCPLS was then used to process class models of 1) 117 pure orange juice samples obtained from freshly harvested and then pressed ranges [140] and of 2) 122 pure RCM honey samples collected from six different areas [141].

Nanomaterials, with their unique characteristics such as surface effect, quantum size, quantum tunneling, and dielectric limit, possess many advantages beneficial for fast and straightforward detection (with yet low cost, high specificity, and high sensitivity) of food ingredients. The main goal of food analysis is to confirm food safety, quality classification, and authenticity as well as to discover the presence of harmful substances. Sensing based on fluorescence quenching relying on FRET, PET, or IFE is widely used in the rapid food sample analysis. QDs, capable of fluorescence quenching, alone or combined with other nanomaterials or substances (for example, QDs-porphyrin, QDs-AgNPs, QDs-enzymes, QDs-MIP, and QDs-aptamer composites) are actively researched and showed excellent results in rapid detection of food contaminants, quality and authenticity. However, these nano-sensors still possess a significant amount of obstacles towards wide-spread commercialization, and are still in their laboratory and/or pre-production stages mostly due to their poor reproducibility and scalability. The shape, size and function of nanomaterial are easily affected by the temperature, time, raw materials and operation mode during the synthesis process, which made some nanomaterials with special functions, can only be synthesized by many attempts in a small system. In order to improve the precision of reaction conditions and the uniformity of mixing raw materials in synthesis process, continuous microreaction systems are often used to replace the big system to achieve high quality nanomaterials, but it is mainly used for the nanomaterials synthesized by solvent thermal method, rather than used for the hydrothermal method, which commonly used in the rapid detection of food systems. Even though the commercialization of these sensors, especially the visible paper-based sensors, is currently an ambitious challenge, we believe that the rapid development of nanoscience and technology will soon bring these sensors to a more wide-spread global path.
The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hnegye Chen receivd his Bachelor degree from Huazhong Agricultural University in 2012 and obtained PhD degree under the supervision of Prof. Fusheng Chen in the College of Food Science & Technology of Huazhong Agricultural University in 2018. From 2016.10 to 2017.10, he worked as a joint Ph.D. student in University of Geneva (Switzerland). Currently, he joined Prof. Haiyan Fu’s research group at School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South Central University for Nationalities. His research involves in fluorescence sensors and rapid detection methods for food safety.

Lei Zhang received his Bachelor degree from Zhejiang University of Technology in 2016 and he is a first-year Ph.D. student supervised by Prof. Yuanbin She at State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology. His research interesting focuses on fluorescence chemosensors and rapid detection methods for green tea.

Ying Hu received her Bachelor degree from East China University of Science and Technology (Shanghai, 2011) and PhD degree under the supervision of Prof. Juyoung Yoon in the Department of Chemistry and Nano Science at Ewha Womans University (South Korea, 2017). She is currently joined Prof. Yuanbin She’s research group at Zhejiang University of Technology (Hangzhou) as a postdoctoral fellow. Her research interests include fluorescent chemosensors and molecular recognition.

Prof. Chunsong Zhou is the dean of Gaocheng Research Institute, College of Chemical Engineering at Zhejiang University of Technology, and the chairman of International Environmental Protection City Technology Limited Company. He receivd his Bachelor degree from Zhejiang University of Technology in 1995, receivd his Master degree from Dalian University of Technology in 1998, and obtained his Ph.D. from Huazhong University of Science and Technology in 2001. His research interest is catalytic oxidation of nanomaterials.

Wei Lan received her Bachelor degree from Wuhan University in 2004 and obtained her Ph.D. from Northwest University in 2013. Currently, she works in School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South Central University for Nationalities. Her research involves in quality control of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM).

Prof. Haiyan Fu is a member of the Academic Committee and the head of the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis of South-central University for Nationalities. She received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from Hunan University in 2010. Then she works in the School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South Central University for Nationalities and became a full professor in 2017. Her research mainly focuses on the development and application of new methods for drug and food security through chemometrics combined with nanometer materials and spectroscopic techniques. Prof. Fu has over 90 publications, including Biosen. Bioelectron., Carbon, Sensor Actuat. B: Chem., Anal. Chem., Food Chem., Anal. Chim. Acta., etc.

Prof. Yuanbin She is the dean and professor of College of Chemical Engineering at Zhejiang University of Technology (P. R. China). He received his Ph.D. in engineering from Dalian University of Technology (P. R. China) in 1997. He then worked at Beijing University of Technology (P. R. China) until 2014 before moving to Zhejiang University of Technology. His research mainly focuses on the molecule design, synthesis and application of metalloporphyrins and biometic catalysis.
